Stop spreading false information you heard on the internet.
​Most STIs (like Gonorrhea or Chlamydia) have evolved alongside humans for millions of years. They are simply specialized bacteria that adapted to live in the human body.
Some diseases, like HIV, did jump from animals (chimps) to humans. However, this happened through hunting and butchering (blood-to-blood contact), not sexual acts.
​Environmental Adaptation also played its part, diseases like Syphilis likely evolved from non-sexual skin diseases. As humans moved to colder climates and wore more clothes, the bacteria adapted to survive in the only warm, moist areas left: the mucous membranes.
​In short: It’s about biology, evolution, and accidental contact during hunting—not "immoral" behavior. Maybe do basic research first, before claiming theories without any scientific foundation🤦‍♀️

@l_noelle_l Origins of major STDs:
Gonorrhea – Humans likely acquired Neisseria gonorrhoeae from cattle ancestors at some point in evolutionary history, through close contact with domesticated animals.
Syphilis – The most debated origin. The leading theory is that Treponema pallidum came to Europe from the Americas after 1492, possibly linked to animal-origin treponemal bacteria. Some evidence points to cattle or other animals.
HPV (Human Papillomavirus) – Has deep evolutionary roots shared across many animal species; humans and animals have their own distinct strains that co-evolved separately.
HIV – Almost certainly originated from simian immunodeficiency virus (SIV) in chimpanzees and other primates, transmitted to humans through contact with infected blood (e.g., bushmeat hunting). This is one of the clearest animal-to-human (zoonotic) transmission stories.
Herpes (HSV-2) – Genetic evidence suggests humans may have acquired HSV-2 from ancestors of chimpanzees or other primates hundreds of thousands of years ago.

Stop spreading false information you heard on the internet.
​Most STIs (like Gonorrhea or Chlamydia) have evolved alongside humans for millions of years. They are simply specialized bacteria that adapted to live in the human body.
Some diseases, like HIV, did jump from animals (chimps) to humans. However, this happened through hunting and butchering (blood-to-blood contact), not sexual acts.
​Environmental Adaptation also played its part, diseases like Syphilis likely evolved from non-sexual skin diseases. As humans moved to colder climates and wore more clothes, the bacteria adapted to survive in the only warm, moist areas left: the mucous membranes.
​In short: It’s about biology, evolution, and accidental contact during hunting—not "immoral" behavior. Maybe do basic research first, before claiming theories without any scientific foundation🤦‍♀️

“Is Epstein Alive?” A viral highway video from South Florida shows a man driving a convertible who looks strikingly similar to Jeffrey Epstein, reigniting wild online speculation. As the camera notices him, the driver appears to hide his face, fueling curiosity and debate across social media. However, fact-checks say the man is an unidentified lookalike and there is no evidence Epstein is alive. ￼

#Indiawire #Indianawaam #Thewakeupmedia #ViralVideo BreakingNews

Source: Hindustan Times / Economic Times (Fact-check reports on the viral Florida video) ￼

38 Million+ views 🥳 Follow (us) @MEMESZAB for daily viral reels 

They not gonna watch movies again 😭

In this reel, a small guy confidently starts recording a casual family moment, saying they’re all sitting together watching a movie with his brother and mother, expecting a normal, wholesome scene to share. He flips the camera toward the TV to show what they’re watching, proudly trying to capture the vibe, not realizing what’s about to happen next. The timing could not have been worse, because the film El Practicante suddenly jumps into an unexpected scene, completely changing the mood in seconds.

What was meant to be an innocent recording instantly turns into chaos as shock hits the room, the silence gets louder than the movie, and panic energy takes over while he scrambles to react. The scream, the awkwardness, and the realization of “why did I record this now?” make the moment painfully funny and way too relatable. It’s that classic situation where technology, confidence, and terrible timing combine to create a memory no one asked for but no one will ever forget.
